title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Loss
meta_description: Learn to trust your inner voice when grief drowns out the noise. This guide shows you how intuition becomes your compass through loss, not a replacement for healing but a tool to carry the weight. No platitudes. Just real steps to listen deeper when everything falls apart.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Loss
og_description: Learn to trust your inner voice when grief drowns out the noise. This guide shows you how intuition becomes your compass through loss, not a replacement for healing but a tool to carry the weight. No platitudes. Just real steps to listen deeper when everything falls apart.
body:
How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Loss

Loss splits your world into before and after. One moment you're standing in solid ground, the next you're falling through a trapdoor you didn't know was there. And everyone around you starts handing you scripts. "Stay strong." "They're in a better place." "Time heals all wounds." But you feel none of that. You feel hollow, raw, and maybe a little insane because your heart is screaming something the rest of the world can't hear.

That screaming? That's your intuition. It's not broken. It's not wrong. It's the part of you that knows loss isn't something you get over - it's something you learn to carry. And if you've been told your whole life that you feel too much, that you're too sensitive, that you need to toughen up - I'm telling you right now: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. That's not a flaw. It's the exact tool you need right now.

Let's talk about how to actually use intuition when dealing with loss. Not the fluffy, "just trust the universe" version. The real one. The one that gets you through the night.

Why Your Intuition Goes Silent After Loss

You might be thinking, "Kalesh, I can't even feel my own feet right now, let alone some mystical inner knowing. My intuition is gone." I hear you. And you're not wrong - it feels gone. But here's what's actually happening: your nervous system is in survival mode. When you lose someone or something essential - a person, a relationship, a version of yourself - your brain treats it like a physical injury. It floods you with cortisol, numbs your emotional receptors, and shuts down the higher-level processing that intuition runs on.

Think of it like this. If you broke your leg, you wouldn't expect to run a marathon the next day. You'd need to stabilize, rest, and slowly rebuild. Your intuitive sensitivity works the same way. It doesn't disappear - it goes into hibernation because you're not safe enough to use it yet. The first step isn't to force your intuition awake. It's to make your nervous system believe you're safe enough to feel again.

And that takes time. Real time. Not "I'll be fine in a week" time. Grief doesn't follow a calendar. It follows its own weather patterns. Some days you'll be fine. Other days you'll be sobbing in the cereal aisle because a song comes on the grocery store speakers. That's not weakness. That's your intuition trying to process something your mind can't handle yet. Let it. Don't fight it. Right?!

"Grief doesn't break your intuition - it strips away everything that isn't real. What's left is the only voice that matters: the one that knows how to carry what you've lost."

The Difference Between Grief and Intuitive Knowing

Here's where it gets tricky. When you're in the thick of loss, everything feels like truth. The sadness, the anger, the regret, the "what if I had done something differently" - it all hits with the same force. And your intuition, when it starts to come back, can get tangled up in that emotional noise. You might think you're getting a clear message when you're actually just replaying your pain on a loop.

So how do you tell the difference? Let me give you a simple test. Grief feels tight, circular, and repetitive. It's the same thought running through your head at 3 AM: "I should have called them back." "I should have said I love you one more time." "If only I had been there." Intuition, on the other hand, feels like a release. It's a breath you didn't know you were holding. It's a quiet "it's okay" that comes from nowhere and settles into your chest like a warm stone.

Grief wants you to stay stuck. Intuition wants you to move - even if it's just an inch. Grief says "you failed." Intuition says "you loved, and that matters." Does that land? One keeps you looking backward. The other helps you find your footing in the present.

When you're not sure which is which, don't try to figure it out with your mind. Your mind is a liar right now - it's full of grief chemicals and survival instincts. Instead, check your body. Grief clenches - your jaw tightens, your shoulders go up to your ears, your stomach knots. Intuition opens - your chest softens, your breath deepens, your hands relax. It's subtle, but it's there. Trust the body over the brain every time.

How to Listen When You Can't Hear Anything

Let's be real. Some days you won't feel anything. Just emptiness. That's normal. That's part of the process. But even in the silence, your intuition is still working. It's just speaking in a language you forgot how to understand. Here's how to start tuning back in.

1. Stop Looking for Answers

I know this sounds backwards. You're dealing with loss - of course you want answers. Why did this happen? What was the point? Where do I go from here? But here's the thing: your intuition doesn't answer "why" questions. It answers "what now" questions. "Why" keeps you stuck in your head, spinning in circles. "What now" drops you into your body, into the present moment, where your intuition actually lives.

So instead of asking "why did they leave?" ask "what do I need right now to get through the next hour?" Instead of "why did this happen to me?" ask "what small thing can I do that feels even slightly okay?" Your intuition will answer the second question. It won't answer the first. That's not a failure of your sensitivity - that's just how it works. It's a compass, not a history book.

2. Use Your Hands

When your mind is a hurricane, your hands can anchor you. Intuition is not just a head thing - it's a whole-body thing. And your hands are incredibly sensitive. They know things your brain doesn't. So when you're spiraling, do something with your hands. Cook something. Knead dough. Fold laundry. Draw. Write by hand. Garden. Anything that requires touch and attention.

I had a client who lost her husband suddenly. She couldn't meditate. She couldn't journal. But she could bake bread. Every morning, she'd mix flour and water and yeast, and she'd knead it for ten minutes. And in that ten minutes, she'd get a feeling - not a thought, but a feeling - about what she needed that day. Sometimes it was "call your sister." Sometimes it was "go for a walk." Sometimes it was just "sit on the couch and cry." She never would have heard that sitting still. Her hands had to be moving for her intuition to speak.

If you're struggling to hear anything, pick up something physical. Your hands are a direct line to your intuitive self. Use them.

3. Talk to Your Loss

This might sound weird, but bear with me. When you lose someone or something, that relationship doesn't end. It just changes. You still have a connection to what you lost - it's just invisible now. And your intuition is the bridge to that invisible connection. So talk to it. Out loud if you can. In your head if you have to.

Say "I don't know how to do this without you." Say "I'm angry you left." Say "I miss you so much it feels like I'm drowning." And then shut up. Just listen. Not for words - for a feeling. A sensation. A shift in the air around you. That's your intuition picking up the signal from the other side. It might not come as a voice. It might come as warmth in your chest, a sudden calm, or a memory that rises up with a different feeling than before.

This isn't about contacting the dead or getting messages from the universe. It's about acknowledging that your relationship with what you lost is still alive. It's just operating on a different frequency. Your intuition is the radio that picks up that frequency. But you have to turn the dial. You have to speak first.

Why Your Sensitivity Is Your Greatest Asset Right Now

You've been told your whole life that you feel too much. That you're too emotional. That you need to thicken your skin. But here's the truth they never told you: your sensitivity is not a weakness. It's the thing that will carry you through loss. Because loss is not a problem to be solved. It's a reality to be felt. And you are built to feel deeply. That's not a curse. That's the whole point.

When you're highly sensitive, you don't just feel your own pain - you feel the weight of what you lost. You feel the absence in the room. You feel the echoes of conversations that will never happen. And that hurts. Of course it hurts. But it also means you're present for your own life in a way that less sensitive people never get to be. You're not numbing out. You're not running away. You're standing in the middle of the storm and letting it wash through you. That takes courage. Real courage.

And your intuition? It's the thing that tells you when to let the storm pass and when to start rebuilding. It's the quiet voice that says "not yet" when everyone else tells you to move on. And it's the same voice that says "now" when you're finally ready to take a step forward. You don't have to force it. You just have to trust that it's there, even when you can't hear it.

If you're looking for a practical way to start rebuilding that trust, I recommend Sonya Renee Taylor's "The Body Is Not an Apology". It's not a grief book, but it's a book about radical self-acceptance - and that's exactly what you need when loss has made you question everything about yourself. Your body, your sensitivity, your intuition - none of it needs to be fixed. It needs to be trusted.

Three Practices for Using Intuition in Grief

Let me give you three concrete practices. No theory. No spiritual bypassing. Just tools you can use today.

Practice 1: The Five-Minute Check-In

Set a timer for five minutes. Sit somewhere comfortable. Close your eyes. Don't try to clear your mind - that's not the point. Instead, ask yourself one question: "What is the one thing I need right now?" And then just wait. Don't force an answer. Don't analyze. Just wait. Your mind will throw up all kinds of things - "I need a drink" "I need to call my mom" "I need to scream." Let them come. But underneath all of that, there will be a quieter answer. It might be "I need to cry." It might be "I need to eat something." It might be "I need to be alone." Whatever it is, honor it. That's your intuition speaking through the noise.

Do this once a day. Even if it feels stupid. Even if you don't get an answer. The act of asking is what matters. You're training your nervous system to listen again.

Practice 2: The Grief Letter

Write a letter to what you lost. Not a goodbye letter - a conversation. Tell them what you're feeling. Tell them what you're afraid of. Tell them what you wish you had said. Don't worry about grammar or structure. Just write. When you're done, read it out loud. And then listen. Not for a response from them - but for a response from your own body. Does your chest feel lighter? Does your throat feel less tight? Does a memory surface that feels like comfort? That's your intuition processing the loss through the act of expression.

You can burn the letter, bury it, or keep it. It doesn't matter. The point is the writing and the listening. Your intuition works through symbols and emotions, not logic. A letter gives it a channel.

Practice 3: The Walk Without a Destination

Go for a walk with no plan. No phone. No podcast. No agenda. Just you and the road. Let your feet decide where to go. If you want to turn left, turn left. If you want to stop and look at a tree, stop. If you want to sit on a bench and stare at nothing, sit. This sounds simple, but it's actually deep. You're letting your body lead instead of your mind. And your body is deeply intuitive. It knows where you need to be, even if your brain doesn't understand why.

I've had people tell me they ended up at a park where they used to go with the person they lost. Or they found a flower growing through a crack in the sidewalk and it made them cry in a good way. That's not coincidence. That's your intuition guiding you toward the exact thing you need to see, feel, or remember. Trust it.

For a deeper dive into how grief and intuition work together, check out Francis Weller's "The Wild Edge of Sorrow". It's one of the few books that actually understands grief as a spiritual experience, not just a psychological one. Weller talks about how grief opens us to the invisible world - and that's exactly where your intuition lives.

When Your Intuition Says Things You Don't Want to Hear

Here's the hard part. Sometimes your intuition won't give you comfort. It'll give you truth. And truth can be brutal. It might tell you that the relationship you're grieving wasn't as good as you remember. It might tell you that you need to let go of something you're desperately clinging to. It might tell you that the person who died wouldn't want you to stay stuck in this pain forever.

You don't have to act on it right away. You don't have to like it. But don't shut it down. Sit with it. Let it be uncomfortable. Your intuition isn't here to make you feel better - it's here to help you live more fully. And sometimes living more fully means facing the hard truths that grief has been hiding from you.

I remember a woman I worked with who lost her mother. She was consumed with guilt because she felt relief, not just sadness. Her mother had been sick for a long time, and the caregiving had exhausted her. Her intuition kept whispering "you're allowed to be relieved." She fought it for months. She thought it meant she didn't love her mother. But the truth was the opposite - her love was so deep that she had carried a burden that was too heavy. The relief was not a betrayal. It was her body saying "you can rest now." That truth set her free.

Your intuition might say something similar. It might say "you don't have to be the strong one anymore." It might say "it's okay to want to be happy again." It might say "you didn't fail them - you loved them the best you could." Listen for those whispers. They're the ones that will actually heal you.

If you're struggling to trust what you're hearing, Megan Devine's "It's OK That You're Not OK" is a must-read. Devine is a therapist who lost her partner suddenly, and she writes about grief with zero platitudes. She doesn't tell you to look on the bright side. She tells you that your pain is real and valid - and that's exactly what your intuition needs to hear to keep speaking to you.

Letting Intuition Guide You Forward

Loss changes you. You're not the same person you were before. And that's not a bad thing - it's just a thing. The question isn't "how do I go back to who I was?" That person is gone. The question is "who am I becoming?" And your intuition is the only thing that can answer that.

It won't give you a map. It won't tell you the exact destination. But it will tell you the next step. One step. Just one. And then another. And then another. That's how you move through loss - not by leaping over it, but by walking through it, one intuitive step at a time.

You don't have to have it all figured out. You don't have to be okay. You just have to be willing to listen. To the silence. To the sadness. To the small voice that says "keep going." That voice is your intuition. It's been with you your whole life. And it's not going to leave you now.

So take a breath. Put your hand on your chest. And ask yourself: "What do I need right now?" Don't think. Just feel. The answer is already there. You just have to be quiet enough to hear it.

If you're ready to go deeper into trusting your own intuitive voice, take the free Intuitive Sensitivity Assessment here. It'll help you understand your unique wiring and give you a roadmap for navigating loss with the sensitivity you were born with. You're not broken. You're just built for a different kind of journey. Let's walk it together.
